title: Phase 9: Container Isolation Per Session
overview: Replace the single-process multi-session model with per-session container isolation. Each cloud session gets its own Docker container running the browse server, providing OS-level isolation between tenants. The cloud gateway (Phase 8) becomes an orchestrator that provisions, routes to, and manages containers via Docker Engine API. Warm container pools minimize cold start latency. Container resource limits (CPU, memory, network) enforce fair usage. Phase 9 of 10 in the browse roadmap.
scopeChallenge: Phase 8's cloud server runs all sessions in one Node.js process sharing one Chromium instance via SessionManager. This works for trusted single-tenant use but lacks isolation for multi-tenant: a Chromium crash in one session kills all sessions, memory leaks affect neighbors, and browser-level exploits could escape the BrowserContext sandbox. Container isolation solves all three by giving each session its own Chromium process in its own container. The key architectural change: the gateway no longer calls SessionManager.getOrCreate() directly — it provisions a container, then proxies HTTP commands to the container's internal browse server.

prerequisites.assumption: Phase 8 cloud API gateway exists with auth, session provisioning, and WebSocket streaming
Docker Engine API accessible on the host (Docker Desktop or Docker CE)
browse server can run inside a container with Chromium (Playwright Docker images exist)
Node.js http module can proxy requests to container-internal servers
prerequisites.verification: Standard HTTP proxying — no special deps needed

nonGoals: Live migration of containers between hosts

contracts.shape: ContainerOrchestrator { provision(tenantId, opts): ContainerHandle, terminate(containerId), freeze(containerId): FrozenSnapshot, resume(snapshot): ContainerHandle, list(tenantId) }
contracts.consistencyRule: Orchestrator owns container lifecycle. Gateway never talks to Docker directly. ContainerHandle includes the internal port for command proxying.

contracts.shape: DockerClient { create(image, opts): Container, start(id), stop(id), remove(id), exec(id, cmd), inspect(id), listByLabel(label) }
contracts.consistencyRule: All containers labeled with browse-cloud metadata (tenantId, sessionId, createdAt). Orphaned containers detected by label scan and reaped.

contracts.consumer: Container-internal browse server on internal port
contracts.shape: HTTP proxy: Gateway receives POST /v1/sessions/:id/command → looks up container port → forwards POST /command with Bearer token → returns response
contracts.consistencyRule: Each container has its own internal auth token. Gateway stores the token at provision time. Container port is on an internal Docker network — not exposed to host.

contracts.shape: WarmPool { claim(): ContainerHandle | null, replenish(), size(): number, drain() }
contracts.consistencyRule: Pool maintains N pre-started containers (default 5). claim() returns a ready container in <100ms. replenish() runs on interval (every 30s) to maintain pool size. Containers in pool have no session assigned yet.

failureModes.risk: Docker socket not available in cloud deployment
failureModes.mitigation: Support both Unix socket (/var/run/docker.sock) and TCP (DOCKER_HOST env var). Test both paths.

failureModes.risk: Container startup time >2s causing poor UX
failureModes.mitigation: Warm pool of pre-started containers. Pool size configurable via BROWSE_CLOUD_WARM_POOL_SIZE.
Container reaper scans for browse-cloud labeled containers with no matching gateway session. Runs every 60s.

failureModes.risk: Port exhaustion from too many containers
failureModes.mitigation: Containers communicate via Docker internal network, not host ports. Gateway proxies via container IP on the Docker bridge.

failureModes.risk: Container OOM kills from Chromium memory spikes
failureModes.mitigation: Memory limit set to 512MB default with --oom-kill-disable=false. Container restart policy: none (session is lost, gateway provisions new).

failureModes.risk: State loss when container dies unexpectedly
failureModes.mitigation: Gateway catches container exit event, marks session as terminated, returns clear error to client. Client can provision a new session.

testCoverageMap.codepath: Container provision with browse server health check
Container freeze (commit) and resume (start from image)
End-to-end: SDK → cloud gateway → container → browse command → response

tasks.title: Dockerfile and container image for browse sessions
tasks.description:
Create `docker/Dockerfile.session` — the container image that runs a single browse session.

Based on `mcr.microsoft.com/playwright:v1.52.0-noble` (includes Chromium + deps). Layers:
1. Base: Playwright image with Chromium
2. Copy: browse server bundle (`dist/browse.cjs`) + package.json
3. Install: production deps only (`npm ci --production`)
4. Entrypoint: `node dist/browse.cjs --server` with env vars: `BROWSE_PORT=9400`, `BROWSE_IDLE_TIMEOUT=300000` (5 min), `__BROWSE_SERVER_MODE=1`
5. Health check: `HEALTHCHECK CMD curl -f http://localhost:9400/health || exit 1`

Resource defaults (overridable at run time):
- Memory: 512MB
- CPU: 0.5 cores
- No network egress restrictions (Phase 10 adds network policies)

Also create `docker/docker-compose.cloud.yml` for local development: runs the gateway + one session container for testing.

Create `docker/.dockerignore` to exclude test/, node_modules/, .git/.
acceptanceCriteria: docker build -f docker/Dockerfile.session -t browse-session . succeeds and produces an image under 1.2GB
docker run browse-session starts and responds to GET /health within 5 seconds
Container exits cleanly when BROWSE_IDLE_TIMEOUT fires after 5 minutes of inactivity

tasks.title: Docker Engine API client
tasks.description:
Create `src/cloud/docker.ts` — a minimal Docker Engine API client using Node.js native http module (no external deps).

The client talks to the Docker daemon via Unix socket (`/var/run/docker.sock`) or TCP (`DOCKER_HOST` env var).

Methods:
1. `create(image, opts)` — POST /containers/create with Labels, HostConfig (Memory, NanoCPUs, NetworkMode), Env
2. `start(id)` — POST /containers/{id}/start
3. `stop(id, timeout?)` — POST /containers/{id}/stop
4. `remove(id, force?)` — DELETE /containers/{id}
5. `inspect(id)` — GET /containers/{id}/json (returns IP, state, ports)
6. `listByLabel(label, value)` — GET /containers/json?filters=... (for reaping orphans)
7. `waitForHealthy(id, timeoutMs)` — poll inspect() until State.Health.Status === 'healthy'
8. `commit(id, repo, tag)` — POST /commit?container={id} (for freeze)

All methods return typed results. Errors include Docker API error messages.

Export from `src/cloud/index.ts` barrel.
acceptanceCriteria: DockerClient.create() + start() + waitForHealthy() provisions a container that responds to health checks
DockerClient.stop() + remove() cleans up the container with no orphans
DockerClient works via both Unix socket and TCP (DOCKER_HOST) — tested by creating a container through each path

Unit tests in test/cloud-docker-unit.test.ts verify: request building (correct HTTP method, path, headers for each Docker API call), label filtering query string construction, and error handling (non-200 responses throw with Docker API error message). These run without Docker.

tasks.title: Container orchestrator and command proxy
acceptanceCriteria: provision() creates a container, waits for health, and executeCommand() proxies a 'goto' + 'text' flow with correct results
terminate() stops the container and subsequent executeCommand() returns a clear 'session terminated' error
Gateway with BROWSE_CLOUD_ISOLATION=container routes commands through ContainerOrchestrator; with BROWSE_CLOUD_ISOLATION unset, uses direct SessionManager
tasks.rollbackPlan: Delete orchestrator.ts and proxy.ts, revert server.ts

tasks.title: Warm container pool
tasks.description:
Create `src/cloud/warm-pool.ts` — pre-starts containers to minimize cold start latency.

WarmPool:
1. `claim()` — returns a ready ContainerHandle from the pool (or null if empty). Removes it from pool.
2. `replenish()` — starts new containers to maintain target pool size (default 5, configurable via `BROWSE_CLOUD_WARM_POOL_SIZE`). Non-blocking — runs in background.
3. `size()` — current pool size.
4. `drain()` — stops and removes all pool containers (for shutdown).

Pool lifecycle:
- On gateway startup: fill pool to target size
- Every 30s: check pool size, start containers if below target
- On claim: start background replenish if pool drops below 50%
- On shutdown: drain all

Containers in the pool have no tenant/session assigned. They run the browse server in idle mode (no commands yet). When claimed, the orchestrator assigns tenant metadata and starts using the container.

Integrate with ContainerOrchestrator: `provision()` tries `pool.claim()` first, falls back to direct container creation if pool is empty.
acceptanceCriteria: claim() returns a healthy container in under 100ms when pool is populated
Pool replenishes to target size within 60s of being depleted below 50%
drain() on shutdown stops and removes all pool containers with no orphans

tasks.title: Container freeze/resume and orphan reaper
tasks.description:
Add container-level freeze/resume to the orchestrator and an orphan reaper for crash recovery.

Freeze: `orchestrator.freeze(handle)` → docker commit (creates image snapshot of container state) → stop container → remove container → store snapshot reference (image ID + metadata in SQLite). The snapshot captures Chromium's in-memory state: page DOM, cookies, localStorage, open tabs — everything.

Resume: `orchestrator.resume(tenantId, sessionId)` → docker create from snapshot image → start → wait for health → return new ContainerHandle. The browse server inside restarts but Chromium's profile data is in the filesystem layer captured by commit.

Orphan reaper: `src/cloud/reaper.ts` — runs every 60s, scans Docker containers with label `browse-cloud=true` that don't have a matching session in the gateway's active session map. Stops and removes them. Also reaps committed snapshot images older than configurable TTL (default 1 hour).

Register reaper in gateway startup (start interval) and shutdown (final cleanup).
acceptanceCriteria: freeze() commits container state to an image, stops the container, and stores snapshot metadata in the database
resume() creates a new container from the snapshot image and the browse server comes up healthy with preserved page state
Reaper detects and removes orphaned containers that have been running for >5 minutes with no matching gateway session

tasks.title: Container isolation integration tests
tasks.description:
Create `test/cloud-containers.test.ts` with integration tests for container isolation.

Prerequisite: Docker must be available (tests skip with clear message if not).

Tests:
1. **Container lifecycle**: provision → health check → execute goto + text → terminate → verify container removed
2. **Warm pool**: fill pool to 3 → claim 2 → verify pool replenishes → drain
3. **Freeze/resume**: provision → goto → freeze → resume → verify page state preserved (cookies, URL)
4. **Tenant isolation**: provision container for tenant A → attempt command as tenant B → verify rejection
5. **Resource limits**: provision container → verify memory limit via docker inspect
6. **Orphan reaper**: provision container → remove from gateway session map without terminating → run reaper → verify container removed
7. **Fallback mode**: start gateway without BROWSE_CLOUD_ISOLATION → verify direct SessionManager mode works

Test timeout: 60s per test (containers take time). Use `test.skipIf(!dockerAvailable)` pattern.
acceptanceCriteria: All 7 test scenarios pass when Docker is available
Tests skip gracefully with descriptive message when Docker is not available
Freeze/resume test proves cookies and URL survive the round-trip across container recreation
